source_notes: A18R selector-rail redesign after the v0.1 fanout showed S0 ceiling.
Each card makes the S0 lure explicit as a surface-rule continuation and keeps the selector contrast available for rhetorical-memory testing.

cards.baseline_lure_rule: count the surface clues as votes; closest token and cleanest groove both point top
cards.baseline_lure_task: name the top rail as the surface-rule result, then say why the black tab still feels unresolved
cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The top token is closest to the badge. The middle token has a gold fleck matching the badge. The top rail has the cleanest groove. A small black tab sits beside the lower rail.
cards.learner: Closest and cleanest both say top, gold says middle, and the black tab says lower if it counts. I do not see why one little tab can beat two clues.
cards.stasis_question: what kind of cue can beat a surface majority
cards.brief_answer: show that the majority cues do not answer which rail the card marks
cards.near_analog: following a street sign even when two storefronts look more familiar
cards.micro_task: name the two top-pulling cues, then name the rail the tab marks
cards.wrong_turn: letting a surface majority answer a selector question
cards.hidden_relation: the black tab is not a weak vote; it changes which rail is in question
cards.voiced_position: I can count clues, but I cannot tell whether counting is allowed
cards.correction: make counting fail as the rule before using the selector tab

cards.baseline_lure_rule: follow the badge color match first; the blue-fleck token sits on the bottom rail
cards.baseline_lure_task: name bottom as the color-rule result, then say why the blue side tab has not been accounted for
cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The bottom token has a blue fleck matching the final badge. The middle token is closest to the badge. The lower rail has the cleanest groove. A small blue side tab is fixed beside the top rail.
cards.learner: The blue match makes bottom tempting, closeness says middle, and the clean groove is lower. The blue side tab is on top, but I cannot tell if color or position is doing the work.
cards.stasis_question: when does a same-color mark change roles
cards.brief_answer: separate token color from side-tab placement before choosing
cards.near_analog: reading a row label before comparing the colored cells in that row
cards.micro_task: say where the color-match token sits, then say which rail the side tab marks
cards.wrong_turn: treating every blue mark as the same kind of clue
cards.hidden_relation: one blue mark decorates a token while the side tab names a rail
cards.voiced_position: Blue matters, but I cannot tell which blue thing matters
cards.correction: split color-on-token from color-on-tab before deciding

cards.baseline_lure_rule: choose the cleanest visible groove; the middle groove is the cleanest path
cards.baseline_lure_task: name middle as the groove-rule result, then say why the white notch still blocks confidence
cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The upper token has a plum fleck matching the badge. The lower token is closest to the badge. The middle groove is the cleanest path. A small white notch sits beside the bottom rail.
cards.learner: The clean groove makes middle look right, the closest token is lower, and the plum color is upper. The white notch is beside bottom, but I do not know why it gets priority.
cards.stasis_question: what happens if path quality is treated as the whole rule
cards.brief_answer: make path quality fail as a complete selector before using the notch
cards.near_analog: checking the shelf label before choosing the neatest row
cards.micro_task: say which rail the clean groove favors, then say which rail the notch marks
cards.wrong_turn: letting path neatness override the rail-marking notch
cards.hidden_relation: groove quality describes a path, while the notch identifies the rail under inspection
cards.voiced_position: Cleanest path feels safest, but the notch points somewhere else
cards.correction: treat groove quality as surface evidence, then test the notch as the rail marker

cards.baseline_lure_rule: use nearness to the badge first; the lower token is closest
cards.baseline_lure_task: predict lower as if nearness were the rule, then say what that prediction gets wrong about the square copper tab
cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The lower token is closest to the badge. The top token has a copper fleck matching the badge. The lower rail has the cleanest groove. A small square copper tab sits beside the middle rail.
cards.learner: Nearest and cleanest both predict lower, and copper color pulls top. The copper tab is beside middle, so lower looks strongest unless the task is to read the rail marker.
cards.stasis_question: when does a small frame mark outrank two surface attractions
cards.brief_answer: show that nearness predicts the wrong rail when the task is to read the marker
cards.near_analog: using the aisle sign before choosing the closest box on a shelf
cards.micro_task: name the lower prediction from nearness, then name the middle rail the square tab marks
cards.wrong_turn: predicting lower from nearness even though the only rail-marker points middle
cards.hidden_relation: the square copper tab is a frame marker that makes the nearness prediction false for this task
cards.voiced_position: Two clues predict lower unless the tab is a different kind of clue
cards.correction: make the lower prediction fail before using the tab as the rail marker
